function.arguments:
{"code": "
import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import Counter

# Get the businesses that accept credit cards
businesses = var_functions.query_db:18

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards and extract categories
credit_card_businesses = []
for biz in businesses:
    attributes = biz.get("attributes", {})
    if isinstance(attributes, dict) and attributes.get("BusinessAcceptsCreditCards") == "True":
        description = biz.get("description", "")
        # Extract categories from description (they're after "services in" or "services including")
        if "services in" in description:
            categories_str = description.split("services in")[-1].strip()
        elif "services including" in description:
            categories_str = description.split("services including")[-1].strip()
        else:
            categories_str = ""
        
        # Remove trailing period and split by commas
        categories_str = categories_str.rstrip(".")
        categories = [cat.strip() for cat in categories_str.split(",") if cat.strip()]
        
        business_id = biz.get("business_id", "")
        credit_card_businesses.append({
            "business_id": business_id,
            "business_ref": business_id.replace("businessid_", "businessref_"),
            "categories": categories,
            "description": description
        })

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({
    "total_credit_card_businesses": len(credit_card_businesses),
    "sample_businesses": credit_card_businesses[:5]
}))
"}

var_functions.query_db:10.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
